data.body:
PASS 1 review — T1 (Prior-art / novel-architecture justification) [overlay]: PASS, no findings.

This plan is a code-consolidation/dedup refactor (collapse two parallel outbound appliers into one production path) — a well-trodden pattern, not a novel architecture. None of T1's bright-line triggers fire (no external integration, unfamiliar dependency, security/auth surface, novel architectural pattern, performance/scalability target, or migration), so the overlay is effectively not-applicable per its own ANTI-FP guidance.

Affirmations:
- (a) Prior art / not reinventing: the plan does not introduce anything novel; it REMOVES a duplicated path. It explicitly weighs the two established options ("Either delete the dead typed leaf _apply_outbound_update ... or make it the single production path and route the batch dispatch through it"), choosing based on the stated invariant ("no separately-tested parallel that production skips"). Verified against the live code: both paths exist (batch_dispatch.update_one/_mutation_to_batch_dict at batch_dispatch.py:586 + the legacy allowlist filter ~line 383; typed leaf apply_outbound._apply_outbound_update at apply_outbound.py:160), and the capability-drift history (parent 8b25, comments 87e4, links 3f04) is corroborated by the in-code comments.
- (b) Rule-of-Three / anti-repackaging: the parity check ("assert the surviving path applies every sub-op the retired path did ... before the retired path is deleted") is the correct established safeguard for a consolidation, not a repackaged novelty.
- (c) Unverified capability assertions: none — the plan's mechanism claims are grounded in the actual modules named (apply_outbound.py / batch_dispatch.py / applier.py), which I confirmed.
